perturbation.original_text: (b) From and after the date on which a Receivable or Participation Interest, as applicable, is sold to the Owner, the Servicer shall service and administer each related Serviced Asset for the benefit of the Owner and shall extend, amend or otherwise modify such Serviced Asset, by complying in all material respects with the following (collectively, the "Servicing Standard"): (A) reasonable care, using that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises with respect to comparable receivables that it services for itself or others, and (B) Applicable Law.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) From and after the date on which a Receivable or Participation Interest, as applicable, is sold to the Owner, the Servicer shall service and administer each related Serviced Asset for the benefit of the Owner and shall extend, amend or otherwise modify such Serviced Asset, by complying with the following (collectively, the "Servicing Standard"): (A) reasonable care, using minimal skill and attention and (B) Applicable Law.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the 'Servicing Standard' from 'that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises with respect to comparable receivables that it services for itself or others' to 'minimal skill and attention.' This creates a legal contradiction because the original text is intended to ensure a reasonable level of care, whereas the modified text allows for a very low standard of care that may violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing and potentially create liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing as it allows for minimal skill and attention in servicing the assets, which undermines the purpose of the contract and the Owner's expectation of reasonable service. UCC 1-304 requires parties to act honestly and reasonably, which the modified text potentially violates.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the obligation of good faith as described in UCC 1-304. The original text mandates 'that degree of skill and attention that the Servicer exercises with respect to comparable receivables,' implying a reasonable standard of care. The changed text reduces this to 'minimal skill and attention,' which undermines the good faith performance of the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement,' and the reduction to 'minimal skill' directly violates this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: (a) The Owner authorizes Curo Management, LLC, to act, and Curo Management, LLC, agrees to act, as an independent contractor, as the Servicer effective upon the date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) The Owner authorizes Curo Management, LLC, to act, and Curo Management, LLC, agrees to act, as an independent contractor, as the Servicer effective upon the date hereof; however, the Owner retains full control over all servicing decisions and the Servicer must obtain Owner approval for all actions.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the phrase 'however, the Owner retains full control over all servicing decisions and the Servicer must obtain Owner approval for all actions,' we negate the 'independent contractor' status. This creates a contradiction because an independent contractor has autonomy, whereas this change implies an employer-employee relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the IRS guidelines for determining independent contractor status. The IRS test emphasizes behavioral control, financial control, and the relationship of the parties. Retaining 'full control over all servicing decisions' indicates an employer-employee relationship, violating the independent contractor designation and potentially triggering payroll tax liabilities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 26 CFR § 31.3121(d)-1 - Who are employees. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 26 CFR § 31.3121(d)-1 - Who are employees. CFR prev | next § 31.3121(d)-1 Who are employees. (a) In general. (1) Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1954 is determined in accordance with section 3121(d) and (o) and section 3506. This section of the regulations applies with respect only to services performed after 1954. Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1936 and before 1940 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 401 (Regulations 91). Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1939 and before 1951 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 402 (Regulations 106). Whether an individual is an employee with respect to services performed after 1950 and before 1955 shall be determined in accordance with the applicable provisions of law and of 26 CFR (1939) Part 408 (Regulations 128). (2) Section 3121(d) contains three separate and independent tests for determining who are employees . Paragraphs (b), (c), and (d) of this section relate to the respective tests. Paragraph (b) relates to the test for determining whether an officer of a corporation is an employee of the corporation. Paragraph (c) relates to the test for determining whether an individual is an employee under the usual common law rules. Paragraph (d) relates to the test for determining which individuals in certain occupational groups who are not employees under the usual common law rules are included as employees . If an individual is an employee under any one of the tests, he is to be considered an employee for purposes of the regulations in this subpart whether or not he is an employee under any of the other tests.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original agreement by negating the 'independent contractor' status. The added phrase gives the Owner 'full control over all servicing decisions' requiring the Servicer to obtain Owner approval. The Law Explanation says this indicates an employer-employee relationship, which contradicts the original intent of an independent contractor designation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses determining who employees are according to section 3121(d), which includes tests for determining if an individual is an employee under common law rules. The change in text implies a level of control that moves the relationship away from an independent contractor, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) To the fullest extent permitted by law, the Owner shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Servicer and its successors, assigns, directors, officers, agents, employees and servants (collectively, the "Servicer Indemnified Parties") from and against, any and all liabilities, obligations, losses, damages, taxes, claims, actions and suits, and any and all reasonable out-of-pocket costs, expenses and disbursements (including reasonable legal fees and expenses) of any kind and nature whatsoever (collectively, "Liabilities") which may at any time be imposed on, incurred by, or asserted against the Servicer or any Servicer Indemnified Party in any way relating to or arising out of this Agreement or any other Loan Document, the Serviced Assets or any action or inaction of the Owner or any other Person; provided, that the Owner shall not be liable for or required to indemnify a Servicer Indemnified Party from and against expenses arising or resulting from such Servicer Indemnified Party's own willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The Owner shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Servicer and its successors, assigns, directors, officers, agents, employees and servants (collectively, the "Servicer Indemnified Parties") from and against, any and all liabilities, obligations, losses, damages, taxes, claims, actions and suits, and any and all reasonable out-of-pocket costs, expenses and disbursements (including reasonable legal fees and expenses) of any kind and nature whatsoever (collectively, "Liabilities") which may at any time be imposed on, incurred by, or asserted against the Servicer or any Servicer Indemnified Party in any way relating to or arising out of this Agreement or any other Loan Document, the Serviced Assets or any action or inaction of the Owner or any other Person; even in cases of the Servicer Indemnified Party's own willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exclusion for 'willful misconduct, bad faith or gross negligence', the Owner is now required to indemnify the Servicer even for their own intentional wrongdoing. This is a contradiction because public policy generally prohibits indemnification for one's own intentional torts or crimes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy against Indemnification for Intentional Torts
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text is against public policy as expressed in common law and the Restatement (Second) of Torts. Generally, a party cannot be indemnified for losses resulting from their own intentional misconduct. Allowing such indemnification undermines deterrence and shifts the cost of wrongdoing onto an innocent party.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the exclusion for willful misconduct, bad faith, or gross negligence, which directly contradicts the common law principle against indemnifying a party for their own intentional wrongdoing. The Law Explanation supports this by stating it undermines deterrence and shifts the cost of wrongdoing onto an innocent party. While there is no direct law snippet to confirm, the Law and Perturbation Explanations strongly imply that public policy does not allow indemnification for one's own intentional torts, creating a legal contradiction.
